name: Domed chapel silhouette
description: A rectangular nave capped by a hemispherical dome rising over the apse or the transept crossing, giving the chapel its characteristic vertical landmark profile. The dome diameter is roughly 30-50% of the building's overall width, and frequently sits on a short cylindrical drum that lifts it clear of the nave roof and admits a ring of clerestory light. The footprint is a longitudinal rectangle, one storey tall at the eaves, with the main axial entrance placed on the narrow end opposite the apse so worshippers approach the dome along the nave's long axis. Common across Byzantine, Renaissance, and Baroque chapel traditions, the silhouette also adapts to Mediterranean shrines and small monumental temples where a single bold dome must read at a distance.
placement_rules: Long axis of the rectangular nave runs entrance-to-apse; dome sits over the apse end or over the transept crossing if a cross-plan is used.
Dome diameter should fall between 30% and 50% of the building's width — narrower reads as a turret cap, wider overwhelms the nave roof.
When height budget allows, raise the dome on a short cylindrical drum (2-4 blocks tall) to clear the nave roof and host clerestory windows.
Place the main entrance on the narrow end opposite the apse, centered on the longitudinal axis to align the approach with the dome.
Nave wall height is a single tall storey (6-9 blocks) so the dome reads as the dominant vertical element.
Reserve the dome interior as a single uninterrupted volume — no floors or partitions cut through it.

renaissance.notes: Pale sandstone nave with a bright quartz dome on a short drum; symmetric pilasters frame the entrance; cornice line marks the nave-to-drum transition.

gothic.notes: Heavy stone-brick nave with a darker dome and exposed cobble drum; tall narrow window slits along the nave; reads as a fortified pilgrimage chapel.

mediterranean.notes: Whitewashed nave with a cyan-concrete dome on a low white drum — Cycladic / Andalusian chapel cue; flat roof on the nave contrasts the dome.
